original_hypothesis.title: ERC721Airdrop claim loop can be weaponized for gas-exhaustion DoS if merkle leaf allows large tokenIds arrays
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ERC721Airdrop.claim verifies a merkle leaf of (user, tokenIds) and then loops over tokenIds to transfer NFTs. If the merkle tree includes a leaf with a very large tokenIds array (by mistake or as part of a bulk grant), the claim may become uncallable due to block gas limits, effectively locking those NFTs in the vault for that recipient. Attackers cannot change the tree, but they can exploit operational mistakes by selecting a leaf with unbounded array size and demonstrating permanent non-claimability.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Airdrop datasets often include bulk allocations. Because this is a no-mempool chain and no MEV assumption, the practical risk is operational dataset construction leading to non-claimable leaves (funds locked), which real attackers can publicize and use to erode trust or to force emergency migration.
deployment_assumptions: Merkle tree includes leaves with large tokenIds arrays
Block gas limit is insufficient to complete the loop transfers
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: ERC721Airdrop.claim performs unbounded loop over tokenIds; MerkleClaimable leaf data includes tokenIds array with no on-chain size cap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i; i < tokenIds.length; ++i) IERC721(token).safeTransferFrom(vault, user, tokenIds[i]);

preconditions: A merkle leaf exists with a tokenIds array length large enough to exceed gas limits
Vault holds/approves those NFTs for transfer
User attempts to claim via this single transaction path (no batching supported)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No batching or partial-claim mechanism; all NFTs in tokenIds must be transferred in one call.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on dataset generation and merkle root content.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During claim window; permanent thereafter if unclaimable

operational_questions: Does the merkle generation pipeline cap tokenIds length per leaf?
Is there an emergency/manual distribution path for stuck leaves?
What is the chain's practical block gas limit during mainnet operation?
validation_requirements: Review merkle dataset sizes and maximum tokenIds length
Assess possibility of adding a batched/partial claim in a future upgrade

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a leaf with large tokenIds list (public dataset)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attempt claim; it consistently runs out of gas/reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Demonstrate NFTs are effectively locked unless admin intervenes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Some recipients cannot claim NFTs; vault retains assets; reputational damage
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium—may require admin-side manual transfers or contract upgrade

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated failed claim txs due to out-of-gas
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unclaimed inventory remaining in vault post-claim window
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Specific users consistently failing to claim

validation_result.impact_summary: Users with a large number of NFTs allocated in a single Merkle leaf will be unable to claim their airdrop due to block gas limit exhaustion, locking the assets until admin intervention.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `ERC721Airdrop.claim` function takes an array of `tokenIds` and verifies it against a Merkle root. Once verified, it iterates over the entire `tokenIds` array, calling `IERC721.safeTransferFrom` for each token. Because `safeTransferFrom` is a gas-intensive operation (involving external calls, state updates, and potential receiver callbacks), a large array of `tokenIds` will cause the transaction to exceed the block gas limit. Since the Merkle leaf hash is strictly tied to the exact `tokenIds` array, users cannot claim a subset of their tokens. If an operational mistake occurs during the off-chain Merkle tree generation (e.g., a bulk grant to a single user), the affected user will be permanently unable to claim their NFTs unless the contract owner intervenes by generating and setting a new Merkle root with split allocations.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerability is clearly visible in `ERC721Airdrop.sol`. The `claim` function contains an unbounded `for` loop over the user-supplied `tokenIds` array. There is no mechanism to process partial claims or paginate the transfers. The `_verifyClaim` function enforces that the exact full array must be provided to match the Merkle leaf.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The loop iterates over `tokenIds.length` without any upper bound check. Each iteration performs an external call `safeTransferFrom`, which consumes significant gas. If `tokenIds.length` is large, the transaction will revert with an out-of-gas error, preventing the user from claiming their airdrop.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When `claim` is called, it first verifies the Merkle proof using the full `tokenIds` array. If successful, it enters a `for` loop to transfer each NFT. If the array is too large, the loop consumes all available block gas and reverts. Because the Merkle proof requires the exact array, the user cannot bypass the issue by submitting a smaller array.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Merkle tree includes leaves with large tokenIds arrays
Block gas limit is insufficient to complete the loop transfers
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The provided deployment scripts (e.g., `DeployERC20Airdrop.s.sol`) do not include the off-chain Merkle tree generation logic or the dataset for the ERC721 airdrop. Therefore, it is not possible to verify if the actual deployment will contain leaves with excessively large `tokenIds` arrays.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The hypothesis describes an operational failure rather than a direct exploit by an attacker. If the protocol team generates a Merkle tree with a leaf containing hundreds of token IDs for a single user, that user will attempt to call `claim`. The transaction will consistently run out of gas. The user's NFTs will remain locked in the vault. An attacker could monitor the public dataset, identify these unclaimable leaves, and publicize the operational failure to cause reputational damage.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium. While it does not allow an attacker to steal funds, it causes a Denial of Service for legitimate users trying to claim their airdropped NFTs. Recovery requires the protocol admin to generate a new Merkle tree with split allocations and call `setConfig` to update the root, which causes operational disruption.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a partial claim mechanism where users can specify a subset of `tokenIds` to claim per transaction, while tracking the claimed status of individual token IDs instead of the entire leaf hash.
Enforce a strict upper bound on the `tokenIds` array length during the off-chain Merkle tree generation process to ensure no leaf exceeds the block gas limit.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy `ERC721Airdrop` and a mock ERC721 token. 2. Generate a Merkle tree where one leaf contains an array of 2,000 token IDs. 3. Set the Merkle root in the airdrop contract. 4. Attempt to call `claim` with the 2,000 token IDs and observe the transaction reverting due to block gas limit exhaustion.
uncertainties: The exact block gas limit of the target chain (e.g., Taiko L2) will dictate the maximum safe length for the `tokenIds` array.
The off-chain Merkle tree generation pipeline might already have an undocumented hard cap on the number of token IDs per leaf.
additional_testing_needed: Perform gas profiling on `IERC721.safeTransferFrom` within the target environment to determine the exact maximum number of NFTs that can be claimed in a single transaction.
Review the off-chain scripts used to generate the Merkle tree to verify if array size limits are enforced.
